Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and study for a few hours, but then I'd get distracted or anxious and end up just skimming through materials without really absorbing anything. At 27, I've got more life stuff going on than the younger students, so I'd often study in random bursts whenever I could fit it in around work and other responsibilities. I aimed for around 20 hours a week, but the quality of that time was inconsistent at best.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely look at anything, then I'd have a panic-fueled marathon right before a deadline. I tried to set regular times, but my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid studying altogether, or I'd get pulled into other obligations. It was more reactive than proactive, which I know isn't ideal.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that got me to log in. I'd realize I hadn't checked the course in a while and feel this wave of dread, so I'd force myself to at least see what I was missing. Sometimes a peer from my study group would message me asking about something, and that would remind me to check the LMS. It was rarely because I was excited to engage with the material.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my day the way checking email or social media did. There were brief periods where I'd log in more consistently, usually when my peer support group was active and we were holding each other accountable. But that routine never stuck for long before I'd drift away again.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually useful when I engaged with them because hearing other students' perspectives helped things click. I also found the video content helpful when I actually watched it—I'd let it play while doing other things sometimes. Reading materials were good for my learning style, but I often downloaded them and never came back to them in the LMS itself.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary for assignments and exams. Some of the interactive activities felt like busywork to me, so I'd skip them entirely. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or understanding something I'd struggled with. If it was going to help me on an exam or an assignment, I'd make the effort. But if it seemed tangential or just there to pad out the course, my motivation to engage dropped to zero. I needed to see immediate relevance.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety levels and limited resources, I had to triage everything. Required activities came with consequences, so they got done—eventually, anyway. Optional stuff felt like extra pressure rather than extra opportunity, so I mostly avoided it to protect my mental energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study them on my phone during my commute or when I didn't have reliable internet. I also used external YouTube videos and free resources to supplement what the course offered. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes and textbooks borrowed from the library, none of which would show up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or working through concepts in my head while doing other tasks. I'd discuss course content with peers over coffee or text, working through problems together. None of that deep processing showed up in the LMS—it just looked like I'd disappeared when really I was grinding through material offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for a module, and then not touch the LMS again for weeks. My internet access isn't always reliable, and it's easier to work with offline materials. So my login frequency was low, but I was still engaging with the content—just not in a way the system could track.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety got so bad that even thinking about the course made me want to shut down. When assignments piled up and I fell behind, the shame spiral made it harder to log in and face what I'd missed. Financial stress and work demands also competed for my attention, making the course feel like one more thing I was failing at.
Q13: Completely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by bills and life stuff, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about what was expected in assignments made me avoid them rather than seek clarification. Fatigue made even simple tasks feel monumental, so I'd put things off until the pressure became unbearable.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be logged in, technically 'active,' but absorbing nothing because I was too anxious or distracted. It was like going through the motions without any real cognitive engagement. Those sessions were basically wasted time that looked productive on paper.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety around starting something and potentially failing felt worse than the anxiety of not doing it—at least in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with its own problems. It's a vicious cycle I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without that external pressure, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd have these bursts of frantic activity right before something was due, which is probably why my assignment completion was so low but my exam scores were decent—exams had hard deadlines I couldn't ignore. The LMS became relevant only when time was running out.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sometimes sent me into an avoidance spiral. I'd feel like I'd already messed up too badly to recover, so why bother trying harder? When peers gave me encouragement through the support group, that helped more than formal instructor feedback.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with 19 different online components to keep track of. I needed more scaffolding and clearer pathways through the material. When everything was available at once, I'd freeze up trying to figure out where to start. A more linear, guided structure would have helped someone like me who struggles with self-direction.
Q19: The flexibility was supposed to be helpful but actually made things harder for me. Without clear weekly expectations, I'd let things slide. The peer support intervention was genuinely helpful—having people check in on me made a real difference. But the overall design assumed a level of self-regulation I don't have when my anxiety is high.
Q20: I think that could be someone desperately trying to engage but struggling to produce work. Maybe they're reading everything, watching videos, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up. That was partly me—I'd be in the LMS, genuinely trying, but the actual submissions felt like insurmountable obstacles.
Q21: That's basically my story. I barely logged in, but I studied offline extensively and did well on exams because I knew the material. The LMS activity doesn't capture reading downloaded PDFs at 2 AM or discussing concepts with classmates over text. Someone could be deeply engaged with learning while appearing absent from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and completion of tracked activities, which is useful for identifying students who've completely disappeared. But they miss all the invisible learning—offline study, peer discussions, mental processing time. They also can't distinguish between meaningful engagement and mindless clicking. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in genuine cognitive effort, not just checking boxes. It's when the content matters to you beyond just passing. For me, engagement happened in fits and starts—moments of real curiosity mixed with long stretches of avoidance. True engagement is mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety attacks before logging in, or the hours I spent studying downloaded materials on my phone. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out entirely, or the financial stress that made focusing impossible some weeks. It definitely wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged, I actually cared about learning—I just struggled to show up consistently.
